questions.question_text: Domestic power supply in India is ______________
questions.explanation: The standard voltage and frequency of alternating current supply in India is set to 220 V and 50Hz respectively by the government of India because when power has to be transmitted from a power plant, the biggest challenge is to cut the transmission losses. For this purpose, the current value should be small and potential difference(voltage) should be more. Also losses are minimal at 50 Hz/60 Hz frequency.

questions.question_text: The main reason for preferring usage of AC voltage over DC voltage is
questions.question_html: The main reason for preferring usage of AC voltage over DC voltage is

options.text: AC voltages can be easily and efficiently converted by means of transformers
options.html: AC voltages can be easily and efficiently converted by means of transformers

options.text: DC uses alternators whereas AC uses generators
options.html: DC uses alternators whereas AC uses generators

options.text: DC Batteries are of low voltage
options.html: DC Batteries are of low voltage

questions.correct_option_text: AC voltages can be easily and efficiently converted by means of transformers
questions.explanation: By using the phenomenon of mutual induction, transformers allow us to easily change voltage of AC. This is necessary to cut down poor losses while supplying electricity to our homes.

questions.question_text: Alternating current is so called because

options.text: Voltage alternates between 0 V and 5 V
options.html: Voltage alternates between 0 V and 5 V

options.text: Current is switched on and off alternately
options.html: Current is switched on and off alternately

options.text: Voltage alternates between 10 V and 15 V
options.html: Voltage alternates between 10 V and 15 V

options.text: Polarity alternates from + to – and – to + with time
options.html: Polarity alternates from + to – and – to + with time

questions.correct_option_text: Polarity alternates from + to – and – to + with time
questions.explanation: Alternating Current is the current in which the polarity of source continuously changes on a fixed frequency. So the positive and the negative terminals ‘alternate’.

questions.question_text: In a circuit containing resistance only, voltage and current are
questions.question_html: In a circuit containing resistance only, voltage and current are
questions.explanation: The electrical current that flows through an AC resistance varies sinusoidally with time and is represented by the expression, I(t) = I sin(ωt + {tex}\theta{/tex} ) Now by ohm's law, V = IR So, for the AC circuit, V = IR sin(ωt + {tex}\theta{/tex} ) In a circuit containing resistance only, both V and I have zero phase difference and hence are in phase.

questions.question_text: If {tex}{{\rm{I}}_{\rm{m}}}{/tex} is the maximum current, then the average value of power dissipated in a resistance R over a cycle is
questions.question_html: If {tex}{{\rm{I}}_{\rm{m}}}{/tex} is the maximum current, then the average value of power dissipated in a resistance R over a cycle is
questions.explanation: As in the case with DC power, the instantaneous electric power in an AC circuit is given by P = VI, but these quantities are continuously varying. So the average power is given by P avg = VI cos {tex}\phi{/tex} where {tex}\phi{/tex} is the phase angle between the current and the voltage and where V and I are understood to be the effective or rms values of the voltage and current. Now, RMS value of I is = {tex}\frac{I_m }{\sqrt 2 }{/tex} So, RMS value of V = {tex}\frac{I_{\mathrm{m}} R}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} Therefore, Power will be P = {tex}\frac{1}{2}I_m^2R{/tex} (because cos {tex}\phi{/tex} = 1 for circuit containing resistance only).

questions.question_text: The rms current I rms is related to the peak current {tex}{{\rm{I}}_{\rm{o}}}{/tex} as
questions.question_html: The rms current Irms is related to the peak current {tex}{{\rm{I}}_{\rm{o}}}{/tex} as
questions.explanation: Average value of i 2 over a complete cycle is given by {tex}{\bar i^2} = \frac{1}{T}\int_0^T {{i^2}dt}{/tex} {tex}i = {i_o}\sin \omega t{/tex} {tex}T = \frac{{2\pi }}{\omega }{/tex} {tex}{\bar i^2} = \frac{\omega }{{2\pi }}\int_0^{2\pi /\omega } {{i_o}^2{{\sin }^2}\omega tdt}{/tex} {tex}= \frac{\omega }{{2\pi }}i_0^2\int_0^{2\pi /\omega } {\frac{{\left( {1 - \cos 2\omega t} \right)}}{2}dt}{/tex} {tex}{\bar i^2} = \frac{\omega }{{2\pi }}\frac{{{i_o}^2}}{2}\left[ {t - \frac{{\sin 2\omega t}}{{2\omega }}} \right]_0^{2\pi /\omega }{/tex} {tex}= \frac{\omega }{{2\pi }}\frac{{{i_o}^2}}{2}\left( {\frac{{2\pi }}{\omega }} \right){/tex} {tex}{\bar i^2} = \frac{{i_0^2}}{2}{/tex} The root-mean-square value of the alternating current is {tex}{i_{rms}} = \sqrt {{{\bar i}^2}} = \frac{{{i_0}}}{{\sqrt 2 }}{/tex} Thus, i rms = 0.707 i 0

questions.question_text: Effective voltage V rms is related to peak voltage V o by
questions.question_html: Effective voltage Vrms is related to peak voltage Vo by
questions.explanation: Average value of V 2 over a complete cycle is given by {tex}{\bar V^2} = \frac{1}{T}\int_0^T {{V^2}dt}{/tex} {tex}V = {V_o}\sin \omega t{/tex} {tex}T = \frac{{2\pi }}{\omega }{/tex} {tex}{\bar V^2} = \frac{\omega }{{2\pi }}\int_0^{2\pi /\omega } {{V_o}^2{{\sin }^2}\omega tdt = }{/tex} {tex}\frac{\omega }{{2\pi }}{V_o}^2\int_0^{2\pi /\omega } {\frac{{\left( {1 - \cos 2\omega t} \right)}}{2}dt}{/tex} {tex}{\bar V^2} = \frac{\omega }{{2\pi }}\frac{{{V_o}^2}}{2}\left[ {t - \frac{{\sin 2\omega t}}{{2\omega }}} \right]_0^{2\pi /\omega }{/tex} {tex} = \frac{\omega }{{2\pi }}\frac{{{V_o}^2}}{2}\left( {\frac{{2\pi }}{\omega }} \right){/tex} {tex}{\bar V^2} = \frac{{V_0^2}}{2}{/tex} The root-mean-square value of the alternating voltage is given by {tex}{V_{rms}} = \sqrt {{{\bar V}^2}} = \frac{{{V_o}}}{{\sqrt 2 }}{/tex} V rms = 0.707 V 0

questions.question_text: For a series LCR-circuit, the power loss at resonance is
questions.question_html: For a series LCR-circuit, the power loss at resonance is
questions.explanation: At resonance, the impedance of the circuit is equal to the resistance. Therefore, P av = I 2 R

questions.question_text: Average power supplied to an inductor over one complete cycle is
questions.question_html: Average power supplied to an inductor over one complete cycle is
questions.explanation: In the first half of the cycle, power is supplied to the inductive circuit. In the next half, the inductor dissipates the energy supplied by circuit. Thus overall no power is supplied to the circuit.

questions.question_text: If circuit containing inductance only, the current
questions.question_html: If circuit containing inductance only, the current
questions.explanation: For inductive circuit, {tex}i = {i_o}\sin \left( {\omega t - {\pi \over 2}} \right){/tex} and {tex}E = {E_o}\sin \omega t{/tex} it means that, the current lags behind the emf when pure inductor is present in circuit.

questions.question_text: Average power supplied to a capacitor over one complete cycle is
questions.question_html: Average power supplied to a capacitor over one complete cycle is
questions.explanation: We know that in capacitor current leads voltage by 90 degree. Over one complete cycle, in first quarter cycle, capacitor charges and next quarter cycle, it gets discharged. This will continue in next negative half cycle. So the net power absorb is zero.

questions.question_text: If circuit containing capacitance only, the current
questions.question_html: If circuit containing capacitance only, the current
questions.explanation: For capacitive circuit, {tex}i = {i_o}\sin \left( {\omega t + {\pi \over 2}} \right){/tex} and {tex}E = {E_o}\sin \omega t{/tex} it means that the current leads the emf when perfect capacitor is present in circuit.

questions.question_text: In a series LCR circuit supplied with AC,
questions.question_html: In a series LCR circuit supplied with AC,

options.text: current in L, C and R is the same
options.html: current in L, C and R is the same

options.text: current in L and C is same but different in R
options.html: current in L and C is same but different in R

options.text: current in R and C is same but different in L
options.html: current in R and C is same but different in L

options.text: current in L and R is same but different in C
options.html: current in L and R is same but different in C

questions.correct_option_text: current in L, C and R is the same
questions.explanation: In series circuits, the current always remains same while potential is different across different circuit components.

questions.question_text: In an a.c. circuit, with phase voltage V and current I, the power dissipated is
questions.question_html: In an a.c. circuit, with phase voltage V and current I, the power dissipated is

options.text: depends on the phase angle between V and I
options.html: depends on the phase angle between V and I

questions.correct_option_text: depends on the phase angle between V and I
questions.explanation: Since P av = E v I v cos {tex}\phi{/tex} , the power dissipated depends on the phase angle between V and I

questions.question_text: If V m and I m are peak voltage and current, impedance Z in an AC circuit is
questions.question_html: If Vm and Im are peak voltage and current, impedance Z in an AC circuit is
questions.explanation: Impedance Z refers to the overall obstruction offered by a circuit containing different components to the passage of current. Also, Z has unit same as resistance. So, drawing an analogy with the ohm's law, we get the relation, {tex}Z=\frac{V_m}{I_m}{/tex}
